critic_raw:
Issue 1
Claim: A before-after comparison without concurrent control is sufficient to evaluate the model's impact on mortality.
Why wrong: Before-after comparisons are among the weakest causal designs. Any time-varying factor — seasonal patient mix, staff training, protocol changes, population behavior — can confound the result. This comparison combines multiple confounders simultaneously: season, antibiotic protocol, unit structure. A concurrent control group, even an imperfect one, would substantially strengthen the inference.
Evidence: Identify any comparable emergency department in the same hospital system or region that did not receive the model alert but received the new antibiotic protocol during the same period. Use it as a concurrent control.

Issue 2
Claim: The mortality improvement reflects faster clinical response enabled by the alert.
Why wrong: The mechanism proposed — faster response leads to better outcomes — is plausible, but the antibiotic protocol change operates through the same mechanism (earlier appropriate antibiotics). Without measuring process metrics (time to nurse review, time to antibiotic administration, antibiotic appropriateness), there is no evidence that the model-enabled response speed, rather than the protocol-enabled antibiotic appropriateness, is what changed.
Evidence: Measure time-to-first-antibiotic administration and antibiotic appropriateness before and after model deployment, separately from the protocol change if possible. If these process metrics changed, determine whether the change correlates with model flagging or with the protocol update.

Issue 3
Claim: The analysis focusing on early lactate testers provides a valid and stable patient subset.
Why wrong: The lactate ordering decision is discretionary and context-dependent. After the model went live, clinicians may have been more vigilant about ordering lactate tests on patients the model flagged — creating a sample that is both larger and potentially different in composition from the pre-model period. If the model caused earlier lactate testing in previously non-tested patients, those newly included patients represent a lower-acuity stratum, which would lower the observed mortality rate mechanically without representing improved care for any individual patient.
Evidence: Compare lactate ordering timing, ordering rate, and subsequent lactate value distributions between the two cohorts. If values shift toward lower acuity, the sample changed.
defender_raw: The model is a clinical decision support tool that enables faster nursing review of high-risk patients. The internal validation showed strong prediction performance. The two emergency departments where the model went live are the only available data — a concurrent control was not part of the deployment plan. Using the most recent pre-deployment period as the comparison group is the only feasible option given this operational constraint.
adjudication_raw: The defender's practical constraint is real — the deployment was operational, not experimental, so a concurrent control was not pre-planned. However, the critique's point is that the absence of a concurrent control makes the mortality attribution claim unjustified, not merely imperfect. The defender concedes the design limitation by noting 'the only feasible option given this operational constraint.' This is a concession that the evidence is insufficient for the stated causal claim. The prior-year same-season comparison is a feasible alternative that the defender does not mention. Critique wins.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: before-after without concurrent control is weak causal design — multiple confounders simultaneous
Issue 2: process metrics (time-to-antibiotic) not measured — cannot attribute outcome improvement to alert speed vs. protocol appropriateness
Issue 3: lactate ordering criterion may expand to lower-acuity patients after model activation

empirical_test.condition: Prior-year same-season comparison at same EDs; process metric analysis (time-to-antibiotic, antibiotic appropriateness) stratified by model flag status; lactate ordering rate and value distribution comparison
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Prior-year November-December shows comparable mortality; process metrics improved primarily with protocol change not correlated with model flag; lactate ordering rate or value distribution shifted toward lower acuity
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Prior-year November-December shows higher mortality than model-active period after controlling for protocol; time-to-review correlates with model flag; lactate ordering distribution unchanged
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Some mortality improvement beyond prior-year baseline but multiple process metrics changed simultaneously
